explicit_text: I attend Central Washington University where I study Information Technology Administrative Management, focusing on cybersecurity and administrative management. I also recently completed a minor in digital forensics and incident response. The pandemic really changed how classes were conducted, with everything moving online, which was a challenge but I managed to balance school and work. The campus facilities like the gym and restaurants were either shut down or had limited access, and professors shifted to remote teaching. Despite these changes, I found ways to adapt and even used slow moments at my hospital job to catch up on schoolwork.
implicit_text: I attend a public university in Washington state where I study Information Technology Administrative Management, focusing on cybersecurity and administrative management. I also recently completed a minor in digital forensics and incident response. The pandemic really changed how classes were conducted, with everything moving online, which was a challenge but I managed to balance school and work. The campus facilities like the gym and restaurants were either shut down or had limited access, and professors shifted to remote teaching. Despite these changes, I found ways to adapt and even used slow moments at my hospital job to catch up on schoolwork.

explicit_text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, I worked as a temperature screener at a hospital over the summers of 2020 and 2021, which gave me a unique perspective on the evolving situation. At first, screening was intense with detailed symptom checks, but over time people became more familiar with COVID symptoms, making the process smoother. The pandemic also drastically changed my social life and schooling, with classes going online and many campus facilities closing or limiting access. Although no one in my family contracted the virus, my parents, both healthcare workers, were affected by the new policies and precautions. Overall, the pandemic made me more conscious about health and safety, and I think its impact will stay with me for years to come.
implicit_text: During the global health crisis starting in 2020, I worked as a temperature screener at a hospital over the summers of 2020 and 2021, which gave me a unique perspective on the evolving situation. At first, screening was intense with detailed symptom checks, but over time people became more familiar with the virus symptoms, making the process smoother. The outbreak also drastically changed my social life and schooling, with classes going online and many campus facilities closing or limiting access. Although no one in my family contracted the illness, my parents, both healthcare workers, were affected by the new policies and precautions. Overall, this major health event made me more conscious about health and safety, and I think its impact will stay with me for years to come.
